text: This instructional video depicts the surgical management of benign prostatic hyperplasia with robotic-assisted laparoscopic simple suprapubic prostatectomy. Our patient is a seventy-year-old male with a long history of lower urinary tract symptoms, in addition to urinary retention and bladder stones. Physical exam revealed a very large non-tender prostate without nodules. His post-void residual is a hundred cc's. Creatinine is one point O and PSA eighteen point five with a high serum four case score. Marked here is the PI-RADS III lesion in the right medial peripheral zone on MRI fusion biopsy. Prostate size was estimated at two hundred seven grams. Biopsy revealed no evidence of malignancy. CAT scan shows extent of his bladder stones and prostatic enlargement. Several weeks after his biopsy, he was seen in the emergency room for bilateral flank pain. CAT scan showed bilateral hydronephrosis. He was taken to the operating room for ureteral stents.

After discussing various treatment options, the patient decided to undergo a robotic simple suprapubic prostatectomy with removal of his bladder stones. Optimal candidates for simple prostatectomy is generally reserved for prostates larger than one hundred grams. The suprapubic approach is well suited for patients of concurrent bladder stones or diverticula. Minimally invasive methods utilizing robotic assisted surgery has become commonplace.

The surgeon begins the procedure by placing ports and positioning the patient in a modified dorsal lithotomy steep Trendelenburg position. The robotic arms are equipped with Maryland bipolar forceps, monopolar scissors, and tenaculum forceps. The sigmoid colon is visible below, with the bladder outlined ahead. Incisions are made lateral to the left and right medial umbilical ligaments, followed by transection of the medial umbilical ligaments and urachus. The space of Retzius is developed using blunt dissection and cautery until the pubic bone is encountered, revealing the bladder and prostate. The Foley catheter is manipulated to identify the bladder neck, and the bladder is opened transversely above the bladder neck using monopolar cautery. The tenaculum forceps retract the anterior bladder wall, and the bladder is opened until the lateral limits of the prostate are reached. Bladder stones are removed using a specimen retrieval bag through the assistant port. Ureteral stents, placed preoperatively due to ureteral obstruction by the prostate, are visible.

The surgeon uses tenaculum forceps to identify the posterior bladder neck and begins dissection between the prostate adenoma and prostatic capsule. Bipolar and monopolar cautery are used to control bleeding while extending the dissection plane circumferentially. The prostatic adenoma is visibly separating from the capsule. The dissection progresses anteriorly, and a zero bicoral suture is placed on the anterior bladder as a holding stitch, brought through the anterior abdominal wall. The adenoma is nearly free and is placed in a specimen retrieval bag for removal in two pieces.

The surgeon uses 2-0 microsutures to control the pedicles at the five and seven o'clock positions. The urethra is approximated to the trigone of the bladder using a surgical slip knot, with five interrupted 2-0 microsutures placed. The ureteral stents are removed, and a 22 French three-way Foley catheter is inserted. Hemostatic agents are applied as needed. The bladder is closed in two layers using running 2-0 Vicryl sutures. The first layer is demonstrated, and the bladder is filled with 200 cc of normal saline to check for leaks, with none observed. The second layer of closure is completed.

The surgeon places a closed suction device and removes all trochars under laparoscopic visualization. The assistant port is closed as needed, and the specimen is extracted. The skin and fascia are sutured closed. Continuous bladder irrigation is administered overnight, and the closed suction drain is removed once fluid creatinine levels match serum creatinine.

The patient is seen postoperatively in one week and was without complaints. Cystogram showed no leak and the catheter was removed. He will follow up in three months with a post-void residual, blood work, and renal ultrasound. Final pathology showed BPH tissue without evidence of malignancy.

We have performed twenty-one robotic simple prostatectomies. Median prostate size is a hundred and seventy-seven grams. Median robotic time is a hundred and sixty-five minutes with an estimated blood loss of four hundred and fifty cc's. Median stay in the hospital is one point five days. There have been a total of four complications all Clavian II. All patients are emptying adequately without incontinence and we have not had any bladder neck contractures. Robotic simple prostatectomy provides good patient outcomes with low morbidity.
This concludes our instructional video on robotic simple suprapubic prostatectomy. Thank you.
